text: The rapid proliferation of location-based services (LBS) has enabled untrusted servers to harvest vast amounts of granular spatial data, sparking significant privacy concerns. In this paper, we introduce and explore "geo-indistinguishability," a formal privacy framework designed for location-based systems. This approach protects a user’s precise coordinates while permitting the release of approximate location data—information typically sufficient for receiving a desired service. Our definition formalizes the intuitive requirement of shielding a user’s location within a specific radius $r$, providing a privacy guarantee that scales with $r$ and acts as a generalized form of differential privacy. To implement this concept, we propose a perturbation mechanism that protects the user by injecting controlled random noise into their location data. After demonstrating the practical utility of our technique in an LBS environment, we compare its performance against existing methods in the literature. Our results show that, for a given level of utility, our mechanism provides the strongest privacy guarantees among all techniques that function independently of prior user distributions.
